Notice Of Renewed Motion And Renewed Motion And Renewed Motion For An Order Authorizing Early Discovery And Related Order
Off Calendar May-29-2025 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Plaintiff Miao Hong's unopposed Renewed Motion For An Order Authorizing Early Discovery And Related Orders is GRANTED.

(see word minutes for complete detail.)

Order Submitted.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn; Clerk: M. Macadangdang; Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Authorizing Early Discovery And Related Orders
Off Calendar May-05-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF MIAO HONG's Motion For An Order Authorizing Early Discovery And Related Orders.

Plaintiff Miao Hong's unopposed Motion For An Order Authorizing Early Discovery And Related Orders is DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE.

Plaintiff moves under Code of Civil Procedure section 2025.210. Subdivision (b) oof section 2025.210 reads: "The plaintiff may serve a deposition notice without leave of court on any date that is 20 days after the service of the summons on, or appearance by, any defendant. On motion with or without notice, the court, for good cause shown, may grant to a plaintiff leave to serve a deposition notice on an earlier date." The rule applies to third-party deposition notices for production of business records. (See California Shellfish Inc. v. United Shellfish Co. (1997) 56 Cal.App.4th 16, 21.) A plaintiff seeking early discovery to identify an anonymous defendant must: a) attempt to notify the anonymous defendant of the request, and b) make a prima facie showing of the elements of her claim(s). (Krinsky v. Doe 6 (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 1154, 1171-1172; see also Glassdoor, Inc. v. Superior Court (2017) 9 Cal.App.5th 623, 634-35.)

Plaintiff here has made a prima facies showing supporting his defamation claim. The court, however, is not satisfied with the showing regarding his attempts to notify the anonymous defendant. The writer of the post identifies a specific event, i.e., "[i]t was Thanksgiving night, and we had just participated in a parade in Silicon Valley." (See Hong Decl. Ex. B. at p. 2.) Plaintiff provides no evidence regarding trying to notify the person(s) with whom he participated in a parade in Silicon Valley on a Thanksgiving day or explain why he cannot do so; nor does he deny ever having participated in such an event. Absent such information, the court cannot find he made a reasonable attempt to notify the anonymous defendant of his early discovery request. Such a showing, of course, must be made in the moving papers.

Order is signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/JMQ)
